authoringProvenance.author: OpenAI GPT-5.6 Pro (candidate authoring; not a human reviewer)

items.prompt: Segment AB has A=B=(2,3), so it is a single point. Segment CD is nondegenerate. Which first decision is required in an intersection routine?

options.text: Treat AB as a point and test whether (2,3) lies on segment CD.
Run only the ordinary four-orientation crossing test and ignore the repeated endpoints.
Give AB slope 0 and compare it with CD.
Declare every point-segment pair intersecting because a point has zero area.

feedback.reason: A collapsed segment changes the predicate from segment-segment crossing to point-on-segment membership.
feedback.details: The zero direction vector invalidates the assumptions behind a general crossing test. The correct branch checks collinearity of the point with CD and verifies that its coordinates lie within CD’s bounds. Assigning an arbitrary slope invents a line, and zero area says nothing about membership. The same reduction applies when both segments collapse: compare the two points directly.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.general_orientation_only: The general test assumes both directed segments are meaningful; with AB collapsed, its orientation values are trivially zero and can misclassify.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.assign_horizontal_slope: A point has no line direction, so assigning slope zero creates geometry not present in the input.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.always_intersects: A point intersects a segment only when it lies on that finite segment.

items.prompt: Closed segments AB and CD share exactly one endpoint B=C, and otherwise do not overlap. Under the stated closed-segment contract, what is the intersection result?

options.text: They intersect at the shared endpoint.
They do not intersect because the orientations do not form a strict crossing.
Their intersection is a positive-length segment.
The answer depends on whether their slopes are equal as floating-point values.

feedback.reason: A closed-segment contract includes endpoint contact even when the strict-crossing signs are not opposite.
feedback.details: Many orientation implementations first detect the proper crossing case and then separately handle zero-orientation points lying on the other segment. The shared endpoint satisfies the on-segment boundary and is therefore an intersection. It is not a positive-length overlap. If a problem instead defines open segments or excludes touching, the accepted boundary changes, which is why endpoint semantics must appear in the prompt rather than only in feedback.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.no_intersection: Strict opposite orientations detect only an interior crossing and omit the explicitly included endpoint case.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.overlapping_segment: One shared point has zero length and is not a collinear interval overlap.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.slope_dependent: Endpoint identity decides this case directly; floating slope equality is irrelevant and fragile.

items.prompt: Exact integer segments AB and CD are collinear. Which additional test distinguishes disjoint, touching, and positive-length overlap?

options.text: Compare their closed projections on x and y, using the nonconstant axis or equivalent bounding-box overlap.
A zero orientation alone proves the finite segments overlap.
Equal slopes prove overlap.
The longer segment always contains the shorter one.

feedback.reason: Collinearity places the segments on one supporting line, but finite overlap still depends on endpoint ranges.
feedback.details: Two collinear segments can be far apart, touch at one endpoint, or share an interval. Checking max(min endpoints) against min(max endpoints) on a suitable axis classifies those cases and works for vertical segments when y is used or when both coordinate boxes are checked. Equal slopes or zero cross products establish only the supporting line. Relative length alone gives no position information.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.cross_zero_is_enough: Collinear segments can be disjoint, so line membership does not establish finite overlap.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.compare_slopes: Equal slopes say nothing about where the segments lie and fail for vertical lines.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.compare_lengths: A shorter segment can lie completely outside the longer segment’s coordinate range.

items.prompt: A geometry system receives measured floating coordinates. Two large products in an orientation determinant nearly cancel. Which policy is most defensible?
constraints: The application defines an error budget.

options.text: Use a scale-aware tolerance justified by the error model, or an adaptive robust predicate when the result is near the boundary.
Use det == 0.0 as the only collinearity rule.
Use the same absolute epsilon for every coordinate magnitude without analysis.
Compare two floating slopes with == because division reduces the magnitude.

feedback.reason: Near cancellation makes a raw floating sign uncertain; the boundary must be tied to scale and the data-error model or resolved with higher precision.
feedback.details: An absolute determinant has units proportional to the product of vector lengths, so one fixed epsilon is not invariant under scaling. A normalized or scale-aware tolerance can model angular error, while adaptive exact predicates can increase precision only when a fast estimate is inconclusive. Exact double zero and equal divided slopes both ignore rounding and vertical cases. The correct choice depends on whether inputs are measured, computed, or exact integers.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.exact_double_zero: Roundoff can produce a tiny nonzero value for a mathematically zero determinant or zero for a tiny real one.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.fixed_unscaled_epsilon: A threshold suitable near unit coordinates can be meaningless at 10^12 or 10^-12 scale.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.slope_equality: Division adds its own instability, loses vertical cases, and exact equality remains brittle.

items.prompt: A point-on-line test must handle a vertical line through A and B. Which predicate avoids an unsafe special-case slope?
constraints: Coordinates are exact integers in a safe width.

options.text: Test cross(B-A, P-A)==0.
Test (Py-Ay)/(Px-Ax) == (By-Ay)/(Bx-Ax).
Test only Px==Ax for every line direction.
Assign a floating infinity slope and compare it with ==.

feedback.reason: The determinant handles every line orientation with one exact predicate and no division by a zero x-difference.
feedback.details: For a vertical line, Bx-Ax is zero, so slope division is undefined. The cross product tests whether P-A is linearly dependent with B-A and works equally for horizontal and diagonal lines. Testing x alone is valid only after the line is known vertical, and an infinity token does not solve general exact comparison. Segment membership would add a bounding check after collinearity.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.slope_equals: The baseline denominator is zero, so the expression is undefined.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.x_only: That is a vertical-only shortcut and would accept wrong points for nonvertical lines.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.infinite_slope_token: Floating infinity is an implementation artifact, not a general exact line predicate.

items.prompt: An orientation test returns inconsistent signs for large signed integer coordinates. The code subtracts and multiplies in the input type. What is the first diagnostic question?
constraints: The mathematical formula is otherwise correct.

options.text: Do coordinate differences, both products, and their subtraction fit the exact type used at the moment each operation is evaluated?
Should an epsilon be added to the wrapped integer determinant?
Should the code divide to slopes so the numbers become smaller?
Should large-coordinate signs be accepted as implementation-dependent?

feedback.reason: Inconsistent exact-integer signs are a strong signal of intermediate overflow, including overflow before a later cast.
feedback.details: The determinant combines differences and products whose ranges can be much larger than the original coordinates. Every operation must be evaluated in a type that covers its mathematical result. An epsilon cannot repair a corrupted integer, and slope division replaces overflow with rounding and zero-denominator problems. Explicit range analysis or checked arithmetic turns the hidden failure into a precise contract.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.add_epsilon: Epsilon addresses approximate floating boundaries, not integer wraparound.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.switch_to_slope: Division loses exactness and introduces vertical-line failures without proving the quotient is safe.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.ignore_large_cases: The input domain is part of correctness; implementation-dependent signs are not a valid geometric result.

items.prompt: A polygon has n>0 vertices indexed 0..n-1. At i=0, code needs the previous vertex. In a language where -1 % n is -1, which index expression is correct?
constraints: Index arithmetic uses a type that can represent i-1+n.

options.text: abs(i-1)%n
max(0,i-1)

feedback.reason: Canonical modulo normalization maps the predecessor of vertex zero to n-1 instead of an invalid negative index.
feedback.details: Polygon traversal is cyclic, so the predecessor relation must wrap rather than clamp. With truncating remainder, raw -1%n remains -1. Adding n and reducing again returns n-1. Absolute value gives 1 when n>2, which is the next-near vertex rather than the previous one. This boundary often appears in orientation, edge, and shoelace loops even when all geometry predicates themselves are exact.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.raw_previous: The prompt states that the result is negative, so it is not a legal vertex index.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.absolute_previous: For i=0, it gives vertex 1 rather than vertex n-1.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.clamp_previous: Clamping changes the polygon cycle by making vertex zero its own predecessor.

items.prompt: Order the conceptual stages of a closed-segment intersection predicate that must handle degenerate and collinear cases.
constraints: Endpoints use an exact predicate or an explicitly robust numeric policy.

elements.text: Detect point segments and reduce them to point equality or point-on-segment tests.
For two nondegenerate segments, compute the four robust orientation values.
Accept the proper crossing case when each segment’s endpoints lie on opposite sides of the other line.
For every zero orientation, test whether the corresponding point lies within the other closed segment bounds.
Reject only after neither the proper crossing nor any included boundary case applies.

feedback.reason: Degenerate objects are classified first, then the general crossing and collinear endpoint boundaries are evaluated before rejection.
feedback.details: The proper crossing test alone excludes touching and overlap. Zero orientations must therefore be paired with finite on-segment bounds, while collapsed segments need point-specific treatment before a line direction is assumed. Rejection is safe only after all declared closed-boundary cases are exhausted. This order transfers to robust geometry generally: validate object type, evaluate the generic predicate, then resolve each legal boundary explicitly.
